title: Intervals on One String
description: Play the root and each interval above it on the same string, naming the interval out loud. This builds the connection between fret distance and interval quality.
instructions: Start on A at fret 5 on the E string (or open A string).
Play A, then play each interval above it ascending the string: A (root), A# (b2), B (2), C (b3), C# (3), D (4), D# (b5), E (5).
Say the interval name out loud as you play each note: 'root... minor second... major second...'
Don't try to memorize all 12 at once — focus on root, major 2nd, minor 3rd, major 3rd, perfect 4th, and perfect 5th first.
Then play the interval and try to identify it by ear before naming it.

tab.label: Intervals ascending from A (fret 5 on E string)

variations.label: Ear training: play root, then a random interval — identify by sound before looking

ensembleNotes: Knowing intervals by sound is how experienced musicians communicate quickly. When someone says 'go up a fourth from wherever you are', you need to hear that interval in your head and find it immediately. That skill takes months to develop — start now.
